mnemonics.title: CRAMS - Memory Hierarchy Levels
mnemonics.content: CRAMS stands for Cache, Register, Auxiliary, Main Memory, Secondary Storage - arranged from fastest to slowest access speed

mnemonics.title: FAST - Cache Performance Factors
mnemonics.content: FAST stands for Fetch time, Access time, Swap time, Transfer time - factors affecting cache performance

mnemonics.title: DVA - Virtual Address Format
mnemonics.content: DVA stands for Disk/Frame number, Virtual page number, Byte offset - components of a virtual address in paging

mnemonics.title: RIP - Cache Replacement Policy
mnemonics.content: RIP stands for Recently Used, Invalidate, Priority - remembering LRU (Least Recently Used) replacement algorithm

mnemonics.title: TASRO - Write Policy Types
mnemonics.content: TASRO stands for Types: Write-Through (synchronous), Write-Back (asynchronous), Write-Once (read-only), Read-Only - different write policies

acronyms.usage: Cache replacement policy - discard the item that hasn't been used for the longest time
Cache replacement policy - discard the oldest item in cache
Hardware cache for virtual-to-physical address translation
Hardware component that handles virtual memory operations and address translation
Address generated by CPU, needs translation to physical address
Time taken to access data in cache memory
Time taken to access data in main memory

visualTips.visualization: Picture an inverted pyramid - top (registers) is small but fastest, bottom (secondary storage) is large but slowest. Draw levels: L1 Cache at top, L2/L3 below, Main Memory in middle, Hard Disk at bottom

Imagine a library book system - Direct mapping is like fixed shelf numbers, Set-associative is like shelves divided into sections where you can search within sections, Fully associative is like a librarian remembering every book's location
Think of a student studying one topic thoroughly (temporal locality) then moving to nearby topics in the textbook (spatial locality) - programs exhibit similar access patterns
Picture a TL B as a quick-reference dictionary on your desk - you look up words here first before searching the main dictionary (page table) in the library (main memory)
Write-through is like writing in ink directly to the original document, Write-back is like writing on a sticky note and only updating the original later when needed

analogies.analogy: Think of cache as the refrigerator in your kitchen - you keep frequently used items (milk, water) close at hand (fast access) rather than going to the supermarket (main memory) every time
Virtual memory is like having a very large but slow library card catalog - you can access any book (data) through the system, even if it's not physically in your immediate workspace (RAM)
Page table is like an index at the beginning of an encyclopedia - it tells you which volume (physical page frame) contains the information you're looking for
Thrashing is like a student constantly swapping between different textbooks on their desk, never completing work - too much context switching between pages in memory
Temporal locality is like repeatedly referencing the same calculator during an exam - once you use a data item, you'll likely need it again soon
Spatial locality is like reading a page of a novel - once you read one word, you naturally move to the next word on the same page, accessing nearby memory locations
